attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a five-day history of a painful, swollen foot wound. One week ago, he sustained a minor graze to his foot in a motorcycle accident while in Thailand. The wound initially appeared to be healing well, so he went swimming in the ocean afterwards. The following day, his wound became red, swollen and increasingly painful. He and his wife had limited access to running water at their accommodation. His wife attempted to clean and dress the wound but it has continued to deteriorate. He returned to Australia yesterday and he has now developed fever and malaise. He is up to date with his immunisations prior to travelling.

He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. The wound on his foot is erythematous with surrounding cellulitis, marked oedema, and serosanguinous discharge. There is early evidence of bullae formation around the wound edges.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following bacteria is most likely causing this infection?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a rapidly progressing wound infection following exposure to seawater in a coastal area. The clinical features of rapid progression, marked inflammation, bullae formation, and systemic symptoms are highly suggestive of a virulent marine pathogen.

Vibrio vulnificus is the most likely causative organism in this scenario. It is a gram-negative, halophilic (salt-loving) bacterium found in warm coastal waters worldwide, particularly in tropical regions like Thailand. Vibrio vulnificus can cause rapidly progressive wound infections when open wounds are exposed to seawater. These infections are characterized by intense erythema, oedema, pain out of proportion to physical findings, and often bullae formation. The infection can progress rapidly to necrotizing fasciitis and sepsis if not promptly treated. Risk factors for severe infection include liver disease, immunosuppression, diabetes, and haemochromatosis, though infections can occur in previously healthy individuals. Vibrio parahaemolyticus also is an infection encountered from coastal water exposure but typically from consuming undercooked seafood leading to profuse watery diarrhoea.

Staphylococcus aureus is a common cause of skin and soft tissue infections and could certainly cause wound infection and cellulitis. However, the rapid progression after seawater exposure, the development of bullae, and the severity of systemic symptoms are more consistent with marine pathogens like Vibrio species rather than typical S. aureus infections. While S. aureus should always be considered in any skin infection, the epidemiological link to seawater exposure makes Vibrio more likely in this case.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa can cause wound infections after exposure to contaminated water, including seawater. It typically causes a greenish-blue discharge and has a characteristic sweet odour. While P. aeruginosa infections can be severe, they typically do not progress as rapidly as Vibrio infections and are less commonly associated with the dramatic bullae formation described in this case. P. aeruginosa is more commonly associated with hot tub or swimming pool exposures rather than seawater.

Aeromonas hydrophila is another water-associated pathogen that can cause wound infections after freshwater exposure (lakes, rivers) rather than seawater. While Aeromonas infections can present similarly to Vibrio infections with cellulitis and bullae formation, they are less commonly associated with seawater exposure, making Vibrio more likely in this scenario.

Mycobacterium marinum is a slow-growing mycobacterium that can cause infection after exposure to contaminated water, including seawater. However, M. marinum infections typically have a much more indolent course, developing over weeks to months rather than days. They classically present as a solitary papule or nodule that may progress to form shallow ulcers or spread in a sporotrichoid pattern (along lymphatic vessels). The acute presentation with rapid progression, systemic symptoms, and bullae formation is inconsistent with M. marinum infection.

Management of suspected Vibrio vulnificus wound infection includes prompt antibiotic therapy (typically doxycycline plus ceftazidime or ciprofloxacin), aggressive wound care, and consideration of surgical debridement if necrotizing fasciitis develops. Blood cultures should be obtained, and the patient may require hospital admission for intravenous antibiotics and close monitoring, especially given the systemic symptoms.
learningPoints: Vibrio species, particularly Vibrio vulnificus, are important causes of wound infections after exposure to coastal waters.
Vibrio wound infections can progress rapidly and require prompt antibiotic therapy with doxycycline plus ceftazidime or ciprofloxacin.
Risk factors for severe Vibrio infections include liver disease, immunosuppression, diabetes, and haemochromatosis.
references: Foote A, Henderson R, Lindberg A, Grigg C, Greenfield C, Kirke A, Auret K. The Australian Mid-West Coastal Marine Wound Infections Study. Australian Family Physician. 2017: 46 (12).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2017/december/marine-infections-study
CDC. About Vibrio Infection [Internet]. Vibrio Infection (Vibriosis). 2024. Available at: https://www.cdc.gov/vibrio/about/index.html
